title: Committee-Based Blockchains as Games between Opportunistic Players and Adversaries
abstract: We study consensus in a protocol capturing in a simplified manner the major features of the majority of Proof of Stake blockchains. A committee is formed; one member proposes a block; and the others can check its validity and vote for it. Blocks with a majority of votes are produced. When an invalid block is produced, the stakes of the members who voted for it are “slashed.” Profit-maximizing members interact with adversaries seeking to disrupt consensus. When slashing is limited, free-riding and moral-hazard lead to invalid blocks in equilibrium. We propose a protocol modification producing only valid blocks in equilibrium.

author.Yackolley Amoussou-Guenou*: Université Paris-Saclay, CEA, List and Université Paris-Panthéon-Assas, CRED,France

article.1 Literature:
JEL C72 - Noncooperative Games C73 - Stochastic and Dynamic Games; Evolutionary Games; Repeated Games G2 - Financial Institutions and Services L86 - Information and Internet Services; Computer Software Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Public blockchains aim to achieve distributed consensus on a ledger. The first public blockchain, Bitcoin, relying on Proof of Work (PoW) and the Longest Chain Rule, has to a large extent succeeded in achieving this goal.1The problem is that PoW consumes a lot of energy, thus contributing to greenhouse gas emissions.2Proof of Stake (PoS), whose energy consumption is much lower, is the most likely candidate to the succession of PoW.3While in PoW, the proof that an agent has the right to add a block is done by extreme computations, in PoS it is done by pseudo-random functions weighted by the stakes held by the agent. As written byButerin and Griffith (2017): “In a PoS system, a blockchain appends and agrees on new blocks through a process where… the influence an agent has is proportional to the number of coins (or “stake”) it holds. This is a vastly more efficient alternative to proof of work (PoW) “mining” and enables blockchains to operate without mining’s high hardware and electricity costs.”

Figure 1illustrates the relative sizes (in terms of market capitalization of the native coins) of PoW and PoS blockchains. Ethereum’s switch to PoS in September 2022 vastly increased the share of PoS. Thus as illustrated in the figure, in November 2022, PoS accounted for 56% of the blockchains, versus 44% for PoW.

Fig. 1 Market capitalization of the top-50 cryptocurrencies as of November 27, 2022 Source : CoinMarketCap Open in new tab Download slide

PoS comes in several forms.Buterin and Griffith (2017)explain the two types of PoS blockchains: “There are two major schools of thought in PoS design. The first, chain-based proof of stake, mimics proof of work mechanics and features a chain of blocks and simulates mining by pseudorandomly assigning the right to create new blocks to stakeholders. The other school, Byzantine fault tolerant (BFT) based proof of stake, is based on a thirty-year-old body of research into BFT consensus algorithms… Repurposing BFT algorithms for proof of stake was first introduced by Tendermint.”

The second type of PoS mentioned byButerin and Griffith (2017), is referred to as Byzantine Fault Tolerant, because it is designed to be robust to attacks by adversaries, called “Byzantines.” As written inStork and Crow BV (2018), “Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT) styled variants introduce a multi-round voting process, designed to increase the protocol’s fault tolerance.”

In BFT protocols, committee members can be selected by a PoS algorithm and the protocol then operates as a multiround process: at each round, one committee member proposes a block, the others vote in favor of the block or do not vote and the block is produced if it collects a supermajority quorum of votes.Figure 2illustrates the relative sizes of the two types of PoS designs. The first type (chain-based mimicking PoW) accounts for 3% of PoS blockchains, while the second type (committee-based blockchains building on BFT research) accounts for 97% of PoS blockchains4. The protocol developed by Ethereum, referred as Casper (seeButerin and Griffith 2017), belongs to the second type of PoS designs, relying on committees. Ethereum is the major blockchain for decentralized finance (Defi). For example, the algorithmic stablecoin DAI and the decentralized exchange Uniswap rely on Ethereum. Thus, the consensus properties of committee-based PoS blockchain protocols will be key for the future of Defi.

Fig. 2 Market capitalization of PoS blockchains in the top-50 cryptocurrencies as of November 27, 2022 Open in new tab Download slide

BFT, committee-based, consensus protocols have been studied by an important computer science literature (see, in particular,Castro and Liskov 1999). The major issue in that literature is whether the protocol is robust to the presence in the committee of malevolent agents (referred to as Byzantines, or adversaries) who seek to disrupt the protocol. In the context of blockchains, malevolent agents could be present due to conflicts between countries, or competition between blockchains. For example, they could be trying to disrupt payments and ledgers in an enemy country. Or they could be operating for a blockchain trying to disrupt a competing blockchain.

The computer science literature has established that, under some assumptions, committee-based protocols are robust to the presence of these malevolent (Byzantine or adversaries) agents. As written byButerin and Griffith (2017): “BFT algorithms typically have proven mathematical properties; for example, one can usually mathematically prove that as long as more than 2/3 of protocol participants are following the protocol honestly, then, regardless of network latency, the algorithm cannot finalize conflicting blocks.”

As revealed by the above quote, the computer science literature typically assumes that some participants are “honest” and follow the prescribed protocol. It is under this assumption that the computer science literature establishes that a protocol is BFT, that is, can achieve consensus even if malevolent participants seek to disrupt it. This approach is at odds with the economics approach, which assumes participants maximize their objective function, and uses game theory to analyze strategic interactions (seeBiais et al. 2019;Saleh 2021).

The goal of the present paper is to extend the game theoretic analysis of blockchain protocols to BFT PoS blockchain protocols operating with committees. To this aim, we analyze a simplified protocol capturing the major features of BFT PoS protocols. In particular a committee is in charge of proposing and voting for blocks. Our focus leads to uncovering new problems not yet identified in the analysis of BFT protocols, and to new solutions to these problems.5

As mentioned byButerin and Griffith (2017), the first blockchain to implement a BFT protocol on a significant scale was Tendermint. The workings of committee-based blockchains, such as Tendermint, and their successors can be summarized (in a simplified way) as follows:6

At each height of the blockchain, coinholders stake coins hoping to be selected in the committee in charge of producing the next block.The members of the committee are then drawn by a Proof of Stake algorithm, reflecting stakes.Initially, one member of the committee, the leader (also selected by a Proof of Stake algorithm) proposes a block.The other committee members vote in favor of the proposed block or do not vote.If the block receives a number of votes larger than a quorum defined in the protocol, it is produced and appended to the chain.Otherwise, another committee member becomes the new leader and can propose a block.The process continues until a block is produced.When a block is produced, the committee members which voted receive a reward, but, if the block is found to be invalid, these committee members are “slashed”; that is, they lose the coins they staked.The protocol then proceeds to the next committees and blocks.

To take a preliminary step in the analysis of this protocol, for simplicity we take as given the staking and Proof of Stake selection process resulting in the committee, and we focus on the interaction between committee members once they have staked coins and been selected.7We assume some of the committee members are “opportunistic,”while the others are “adversary.”Adversaries seek to disrupt the consensus and, as mentioned above, their presence could reflect conflicts between countries or competition between blockchains. Opportunistic agents are standard economic agents, trying to maximise their expected net gains. We study equilibria of the game between adversaries and opportunistic agents, and examine whether consensus is achieved in equilibrium in this game.8

In line withCastro and Liskov (1999),Lamport, Shostak, and Pease (1982), andCachin et al. (2001), in our analysis, for consensus to be achieved, the following two properties must be satisfied:

Termination:The committee produces a block.Validity:Produced blocks are valid.

The validity property of consensus requires that committee members verify whether blocks are valid and vote for valid blocks (only). Now, to verify a block, a committee member must incur costC, corresponding to the cost of computations, electricity and computer space. As written byLuu et al. (2015): “While verifying correct block construction requires a relatively small amount of work (two SHA256 calculations), checking the validity of transactions contained in a block can take much more time for two reasons. First, the number of transactions per block may be large… Second, expressive transaction scripts in… cryptocurrencies such as Ethereum can require significant computational work to verify… Once a smart contract transaction gets included in the blockchain, everyone in the network is expected to execute the contract script to verify its validity.”

Sending votes also involves a cost, albeit much smaller. Our analysis is based on the observation that opportunistic committee members will incur verification and voting costs only if it is in their own interest.

The action of verifying a block is a sequence of internal computations, which the committee member performs on its own machine, which is not visible by the others. The vote message sent by the committee member does not contain the information needed to check whether the committee member performed these computations. Therefore, the action of verifying blocks, while costly, is not observable. Thus, there is a moral hazard problem, and the issue is whether the blockchain protocol is incentive compatible, that is, provides incentives to committee members to verify blocks. An important aspect of the protocol, in this context, is the costs and rewards of committee members when valid or invalid blocks are produced.

When a block is produced, the committee members who voted for the block receive a rewardR, specified in the protocol. When an invalid block is produced, opportunistic committee members incur a cost, due to the loss of reputation of the chain and the loss of value of their coins. Moreover, if they voted for the invalid block, the coins staked by these committee members are slashed, resulting in lossS.

We show that, in spite of slashing and rewards, the prescribed protocol is not an equilibrium: when anticipating that the others will follow the prescribed protocol, an opportunistic committee member prefers to deviate from the prescribed protocol. Moreover, there exists an equilibrium in which invalid blocks are produced. These negative results are due to the combination of two problems arising in our setting: First, there is free-riding among committee members who prefer not to check validity, when they expect all the others to check validity, since in that case they are not pivotal. Second, verification is costly for the committee member but unobservable by third parties; that is, there is moral hazard.

The equilibrium in which invalid blocks are produced arises only ifSis below a threshold, which is an application of the general principle that large enough punishments can eliminate moral hazard problems. However,Sis limited in practice for several reasons: First, requiring very large stakes restricts the set of coinholders who can become committee members, which goes against the decentralization philosophy of public blockchains. Second, large stakes are costly because, while they are staked, coins cannot be used for anything else;; for example coinholders cannot use them for transactions, and cannot lend them. Third, large stakes are costly because in practice communication problems, hacks, and bugs can imply stakes are slashed even when committee members are not misbehaving (see, e.g.,Abbas, Caprolu, and Pietro 2022;Interchain Foundation 2022;Edgington 2022).

There also exists an equilibrium with consensus. In that equilibrium some opportunistic committee members verify blocks because they are pivotal with some probability. In that context, if the cost of producing invalid blocks is sufficiently large, the committee members that are supposed to verify blocks prefer to do so, rather than running the risk of producing an invalid block. This leads to the emergence of a “good equilibrium,”without free riding. We suggest a modification of the prescribed protocol, which makes the good equilibrium a focal point, à laSchelling (1960).9
1.1 Computer science

1.1.1 BFT

Our analysis is related to the literature on Byzantine consensus-based blockchains.Castro and Liskov (1999)analyze consensus in a Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT) protocol. To use a BFT protocol in an open setting, such as blockchains, recent research endeavoured to find secure mechanisms to select committees of fixed size over time (e.g.,Gilad et al. 2017;David et al. 2018) and to propose incentives to promote participation (e.g.,Abraham et al. 2016). While, this literature allows messages to be delayed and Byzantine processes to send different messages to different processes (seeLamport, Shostak, and Pease 1982), we assume away these difficulties to simplify the problem. On the other hand, while the computer science literature on BFT protocols focuses on correct and Byzantine processes, we in contrast consider opportunistic committee members and adversaries, which both are rational, that is, maximize well-specified objectives given rational expectations.

1.1.2 Verification costs

Our assumption that it is costly to verify blocks’ validity is in line withLuu et al. (2015),Felten (2019),Kiayias and Stouka (2021), andFooladgar et al. (2020).

Our analysis departs from those ofLuu et al. (2015)andKiayias and Stouka (2021),10because we take a game theoretic approach. More in line with the traditional computer science approach,Kiayias and Stouka (2021)analyze the interaction between an adversary, who might deviate from the prescribed protocol, and honest processes, who are supposed to follow the protocol. In contrast, we analyze the interaction between an adversary and opportunistic players, who follow the prescribed protocol only if it is individually optimal for them. In this context, we show that following the prescribed protocol is not an equilibrium strategy for opportunistic players. Second,Kiayias and Stouka (2021)apply their methodology to study Bitcoin and Fruitchain. This differs from our focus on committee-based blockchains.

Felten (2019) considers the situation in which a verifier must incur cost C to verify the claim made by an asserter. The verifier gets reward R if it establishes the asserter was lying. But the verifier incurs loss L if the asserter lies and does not get caught. Felten (2019) assumes the asserter lies with probability X . Then the verifier’s expected gain if it incurs cost C is: X R − C , while it’s expected gain if it does not check is: − X L .

The former is larger than the latter if X > C R + L .

Felten (2019)concludes from this analysis that the asserter can lie with small probabilityX<C/(R+L)without getting caught.Felten (2019)also remarks that the problem is worsened by several checkers, as each still has to pay the full costC, but gets only a fraction of the rewardR. This is becauseFelten (2019)assumes the reward must be shared among the different verifiers who found out the asserter was lying. Our comparison of the expected gain of a committee member when it verifies the block at costCand when it does not verify the block is in line withFelten (2019). Furthermore, the lossLinFelten (2019)is similar to the cost from slashing (S) and devaluation of the cryptocurrency (κ) in our model.

Our analysis departs fromFelten (2019)because we take a formal game theoretic approach to analyze the strategic interaction between all the players and the equilibrium outcome. We thus characterize the equilibrium beliefs held by each player about the strategies of the others (in particular the probability that other players verify blocks and also the probability that the leader proposes an invalid block) and the corresponding equilibrium actions. This approach enables us to (a) identify that a key feature of the strategic environment is whether or not players are pivotal, (b) uncover free-riding problems arising when players are not pivotal, and (c) suggest a modification of the prescribed protocol that endogenously make players pivotal. These theoretical insights are a new contribution of our analysis.

1.1.3 Game theoretic analyses of distributed consensus

Our analysis is in line with the seminal contributions ofAbraham et al. (2006),Afek et al. (2014),Aiyer et al. (2005),Groce et al. (2012), andHalpern and Vilaça (2016), all of whom took a game theoretic approach to the study of distributed computing and consensus.Groce et al. (2012)consider an environment with rational and honest participants and provide protocols that tolerate rational adversaries, but they take a cooperative game approach, while we take a non-cooperative-game approach. Also, in a non-cooperative-game framework,Halpern and Vilaça (2016), prove that in a fully rational setting, if participants can fail by crashing11, there is noex postNash equilibrium solving thefairconsensus problem (where fair means that the input of every agent is committed with equal probability), even with only one crash.Afek et al. (2014)propose protocols solving consensus and renaming under the assumption that participants may be rational and offer a game theoretic analysis. While we consider committee-based protocols,Kiayias et al. (2016),Kroll, Davey, and Felten (2013), andBiais et al. (2019), study Nash equilibria in proof-of-work blockchain protocols.Abraham et al. (2016)present Solidus, an incentive-compatible committee-based protocol for blockchains, but don’t offer a formal game theoretic treatment.

An important ingredient in models of rational choices is the specification of players’ utilities.Lysyanskaya and Triandopoulos (2006)propose an incentive compatible protocol robust to a coalition of up toffaulty players, when the players’ utilities reflect whether or not a decision is reached, as well as the value of the decision.Abraham et al. (2006)propose an incentive-compatible protocol for secret sharing with rational participants when some utilities can be unknown.Halpern and Vilaça (2020)show there is a Nash equilibrium achieving consensus when an agent’s utility only depends on the consensus value achieved and not on the number of messages the agent sends. In contrast with these analyses, in our model, utilities are known and reflect not only whether and which blocks are appended to the chain, but also the costs of the actions (verifying, voting) taken by the players. These costs make the analysis more complex, but also give rise to new economic effects, such as free riding.

Manshaei et al. (2018)also offer a noncooperative game theoretic analysis of free-riding in committees. In the protocol they consider, multiple committees run in parallel to validate nonintersecting sets of transactions (shards). They show that rational agents can free-ride when rewards are equally shared. Differences between our analysis andManshaei et al. (2018)are that (a) we do not consider shards, but (b) we consider interaction between adversaries and opportunistic committee members in a dynamic game, and (c) in our model due to the presence of adversaries some blocks can be invalid.

Fooladgar et al. (2020)take a game theoretical approach, to study the Algorand protocol, which is a PoS committee-based protocol, but without slashing. They note that, in the Algorand protocol, all committee members equally share the reward obtained when a block is produced, regardless of their behaviour. This implies that following the prescribed protocol is not an equilibrium, but a modified version of the protocol, in which players receive the reward only if they follow the protocol, is an equilibrium. Our analysis differs from theirs for the following two main reasons: First, while their model only features opportunistic agents, ours features opportunistic agents and adversaries. The presence of adversaries creates the risk that proposed blocks are invalid. Second, in their model it is observable whether players follow the prescribed protocol or defect. In contrast, in our model, verification is not observable. Therefore it is impossible to directly make rewards contingent on following the prescribed protocol. This is why, in our setting, pivotality is key to incentives. In contrast, in the equilibrium of the modified protocol ofFooladgar et al. (2020), all committee members follow the prescribed protocol, while none are pivotal.

1.2 Economics

1.2.1 Economic models of committee-based blockchain protocols

In line with our approach, two interesting recent papers,Auer, Monnet, and Shin (2022)andHalaburda, He, and Li (2022), study the workings of committee-based blockchains, in which committee members are expected to verify blocks and vote on valid blocks, and in which blocks are produced when they get a supermajority of votes. As in our paper,Auer, Monnet, and Shin (2022)andHalaburda, He, and Li (2022)assume that verification and votes are costly, but they focus on different mechanisms than us. In particular, a key issue in our analysis, whether or not committee members are pivotal, does not arise in their models.

In contrast with us,Auer, Monnet, and Shin (2022)assume that when committee members don’t verify, they do not receive a reward. Moreover, inAuer, Monnet, and Shin (2022), there are no adversaries. The imperfection inAuer, Monnet, and Shin (2022)stems from the fact that committee members can be bribed into voting for invalid blocks. So, while in our paper the incentive condition requires that committee members engage in costly verification, inAuer, Monnet, and Shin (2022)the incentive condition requires that committee members refuse bribes. Relative to our analysis, the contribution ofAuer, Monnet, and Shin (2022)is to link the ledger validation to monetary exchange, select a unique equilibrium via a global game approach, and characterize the optimal number of committee members, size of transactions, and quorum.

Halaburda, He, and Li (2022)define consensus as the situation in which all non-Byzantine committee members vote for the block. In this approach, (a) all non-Byzantine committee members are pivotal, and (b) consensus does not depend on the block being valid. From the set of messages they receive, committee members try to infer whether or not the leader is a Byzantine agent: either a consensus won’t emerge and validators who vote incur a cost or consensus can be achieved and committee members who vote obtain a net gain. An important contribution ofHalaburda, He, and Li (2022)is to capture aversion to Byzantine (or adversary) committee members as ambiguity aversion. Thus, rational non-Byzantine committee members behave as if Byzantine committee members were always taking the action that is most detrimental to the blockchain. Ambiguity-averse committee members may not vote, because they are scared of the consequences of voting. This generates equilibria in which no blocks are produced, as in our paper, but for a different economic reason.

1.2.2 Voting models

Our focus on pivotality is in line with the economics of voting. Starting with the seminal contributions ofDowns (1957)andRiker and Ordeshook (1968)a stream of papers in political economy, surveyed inFeddersen (2004), note that, when their probability of being pivotal is infinitesimal, citizens should not vote, as long as the cost of doing so is bounded away from zero. The probability of being pivotal is low when the population of voters is large, and in that case rational choice models predict citizens should not vote. For example,Palfrey and Rosenthal (1985)show, in a model in which the cost of voting varies across agents, that, as the size of the electorate gets larger, the fraction of agents who vote decreases, and in the limit goes to zero. Our analysis differs from that literature in several respects:

First, we consider a relatively small group of voters (the committee members), so the force that made voters nonpivotal in the political economy literature is not at play in our model.12In a sense, our point of view is the opposite of that literature:

The political economy literature considers a large number of voters and underscores that observed high voter participation is surprising: with costly voting, basic rational choice implies citizens should not vote.We consider a relatively small number of voters and warn that, in spite of that relatively small umber, committee members can be non-pivotal, which induces free-riding.

Second, the majority rule we consider is not in terms of fraction of the voters (as in the political economy settings discussed above), but in terms of the number of votes, in comparison to a quorum. This restores the possibility that the impact of one vote is zero, in spite of the number of potential voters being relatively small. For example, inLedyard (1984), when nobody else is voting, a single vote is pivotal. In contrast, in our approach, when nobody else is voting, a single vote has no impact, because it is not enough to raise the number of votes above the quorum.

Third, while for plausibility the political economy literature assumes citizens cannot be paid for voting, in our setting, when a block is produced, committee members are rewarded conditional on having voted.13

Fourth, the classical political economy analyses consider voters that are initially endowed with information (see, e.g.,Austen-Smith and Banks 1996;Feddersen and Pesendorfer 1996,1997,1998,1999). In contrast,Persico (2004),Gershkov and Szentes (2009), andSmorodinsky and Tennenholtz (2006)consider agents who can acquire information, at a cost, before voting. In their analyses, as in ours, key issues are free riding and pivotality. Our analysis differs from that ofPersico (2004),Gershkov and Szentes (2009), andSmorodinsky and Tennenholtz (2006), however, because we assume some participants are adversary, while they consider all voters have the same preferences. The presence of adversaries rules out requiring unanimity, which differs fromPersico (2004). Also, our focus on distributed consensus rules out central authorities. This differs fromGershkov and Szentes (2009)andSmorodinsky and Tennenholtz (2006), where a central planner makes decisions based on the reports elicited from participants.

1.2.3 Takeovers

Our analysis is also related to analyses of free riding in takeovers.Bradley (1980)andGrossman and Hart (1980)consider take over bids when there is a continuum of atomistic shareholders. They show that, if the value of a share after a successful takeover exceeds the offer price, then stockholders (who are non pivotal since they are atomistic) will free ride and fail to tender.Bagnoli and Lipman (1988), in contrast, study the case of a finite number of stockholders. In that case some stockholders can be pivotal, which eliminates their incentive to free ride.

While we also underscore free riding and pivotality, the game we consider is very different from the take over setting analysed in these papers. In their analysis, stockholders have only one decision to make: whether or not to tender their shares, and this decision is observable. In contrast, in our analysis, players have three decisions to make (a) whether to propose a block and which, (b) whether or not to vote, and (c) or not to verify block validity. Since verification is unobservable, there is a form of moral hazard, making free riding even more of a concern.
article.2 Model:
We consider a game betweenncommittee members, indexed byi∈{1,…n}⁠, hereafter referred to as players or committee members. Typically, committee members are selected via a proof of stake algorithm, in which the likelihood that a node is selected increases with the number of coins staked by the node.14

Sequence of actions

The committee members interact via exchanges of messages. For simplicity, we assume the system is synchronous. Messages are created with a digital signature, which we assume cannot be forged. Thus, when committee memberireceives a message signed by committee memberj,iknows the message was sent byj. Moreover, for simplicity we assume messages cannot be lost.

The goal of the committee members is to achieve consensus. As defined in the computer science literature (seeCastro and Liskov 1999;Lamport, Shostak, and Pease 1982;Cachin et al. 2001), consensus can be expressed in the three following properties:Termination:every non-faulty agent produces a block;Agreement:if two nonfaulty agents produce a block, they must produce the same block;Validity:a block produced by any nonfaulty agent is valid. In this work, given our synchronous communication assumption, Agreement holds. Therefore, our work focuses on Termination and Validity. Thus, in our blockchains context, for consensus to be achieved, the following two properties must be satisfied:

To achieve consensus, the workings of the committee unfold duringnrounds. At roundt∈{1,…n}⁠, the following sequence of steps are taken:

The committee member with indextis selected to generate a block and send it to the other committee members. We refer to this committee member as “the leader.” The cost of generating a block is denoted byC, while the cost of sending the block is denoted byc.15The leader can choose whether to generate a valid block or an invalid one, or no block at all. If the leader does not generate or send any block, it does not incur costsCandc.16Committee members who receive a proposed block must decide whether to check the block validity of not. Hereafter, we call this action verifying the block or not. Verifying a block is costly because it involves, in particular, the two following costly operations: (1) Each committee members is requested to verify if there is no conflict (e.g., double spending) between the transactions in the current block and all previous transactions in the blockchain. This cost increases with the length of the blockchain and the number of users. (2) Each committee member is also requested to run the smart contracts included in the block, to determine their output. These operations are similar to those necessary to generate a valid block. Therefore, for simplicity, we assume the cost of verifying a block is also equal toC.17Then the committee member decides whether or not to send a vote for the block.18Again, for simplicity, we assume the cost of sending a vote message is equal to the cost of sending a block,c. If no block is proposed, committee members do not have the opportunity to verify or vote. In that case, they simply terminate the current round and go to the next one.If the number of votes is larger than or equal to the quorumν, then the block is produced at roundt. Otherwise, we move to the next round, and the next leader, with indext+ 1.

Prescribed protocol

In the prescribed protocol (e.g., in Tendermint, the protocol used by the Binance Smart Chain or Cosmos, described inAmoussou-Guenou et al. 2018), committee members, when selected to propose a block, propose a single valid block. Then, when receiving a block, committee members are supposed to verify its validity and vote for the block if and only if it is valid. If all committee members follow this prescribed protocol, then the above described sequence of actions leads to the production of a valid block. The problem is that opportunistic committee members might deviate from the prescribed protocol, as explained below.

Players’ types

In line with the BAR model ofAiyer et al. (2005), we assume all players are rational. They take actions that are optimal for them, given their type (described below) and their rational anticipation of the strategies of the others.

We consider two types of players: opportunistic players, who maximize their expected profits, and adversaries who seek to disrupt consensus. At the beginning of the game, nature draws the types of all the players, and thus the number of adversaries. Each playeriobserves its own type,θi, which can be adversary (⁠θθθi=θa⁠) or opportunistic (⁠θθθi=θs⁠). The probability that a player is an adversary is independent from its index, and thus, a priori, the same for allibetween 1 andn.

The number of adversaries, denoted byf, is the realization of the random variable denoted byf˜⁠. We assumef˜takes its values in{1,…f¯}⁠, withνν1<f¯<min[ν−1,n−ν]⁠.νf¯<ν−1is slightly stronger than the assumption that adversaries alone can never have the majority (which isνf¯<ν⁠). It means that a coalition of all the adversaries plus possibly one opportunistic committee member does not have the majority. As explained below, this assumption is key to Proposition 1 which states that the prescribed protocol is not an equilibrium.νν<n−f¯means opportunistic committee members always have the majority. Together these conditions imply thatf¯<n−12⁠.19One could expect these assumptions to imply that consensus is achieved. This is not the case, however. As shown below, termination or validity may fail to hold in equilibrium.

Information sets

We assume opportunistic committee members only observe their index and their own type.20Adversaries, in contrast are assumed to know the types and indexes of all committee members and to perfectly coordinate their actions. We make this assumption to be in line with the computer science, in which adversaries (or Byzantine) are assumed to be very powerful and able to collude, seeAiyer et al. (2005),Castro and Liskov (1999),Groce et al. (2012),Lamport, Shostak, and Pease (1982).21The idea is to test whether the blockchain is robust to adversary attacks. If a blockchain resists the attack of very powerful adversaries, it is very robust.

The information set of committee memberi, at the beginning each roundt, which we denote byηηit⁠, includes:

the observation of the round numbert,the player’s own typeθi, and also wheniis an adversary the types of all the other committee members,whent> 1, the observation of what happened in previous rounds, namely, (a) whether or not a block was proposed, (b) how many vote messages were sent at each round and by whom, and (c) whetheridecided to verify the block, and in that case the knowledge of whether or not the block was valid.

Then, when receiving a proposed block, each committee member decides whether or not to check its validity (verify).btdenotes the block proposed at roundt, andisValid(bt)denotes the additional information observed by the committee member at that point. When the committee member decides not to verify the block,isValid(bt)is the null information set, while if the committee member decides to verify,isValid(bt)is equal to one if the block is valid and zero otherwise. So, the committee member’s information set becomesηHit=ηit∪isValid(bt)⁠.

To verify a block, a committee member must use its own machine to perform a sequence of internal computations. The other committee members cannot observe this machine, or if the committee member performs the computations. Moreover, the vote message sent by the committee member does not contain the information needed to check if it performed these computations. Therefore, others cannot directly observe whether or not a committee member verified a block.

Strategies

At each roundt≥1⁠, the strategy of committee memberiis a mapping from its information set into its actions. Ifi=t, the committee member is selected to propose the block and its choice is given byσησipropose(ηit)∈{−1,0,1}⁠.σησipropose(ηit)=1if committee memberiproposes a valid block, –1 ifiproposes an invalid block, and 0 ifidoes not propose a block. Then, ifi≠t⁠, the committee member’s strategy is given byσησiverify(ηit)∈{0,1}⁠.σησiverify(ηit)=0if the committee member does not verify the block, whileσησiverify(ηit)=1if the committee member verifies the block. Finally, committee memberi≠tmust decide whether or not to send a vote, a decision given byσσisend(Hit)∈{0,1}⁠.σσisend(Hit)=0if the committee member does not send a vote, whileσσisend(Hit)=1if she sends a vote. Overall, the strategy of playeriis denoted byσσσσσi=(σipropose,σiverify,σisend)⁠.

Rewards and costs for opportunistic committee members

We assume that, when a block is produced, only the committee members who sent a vote receive a reward. This is the case, in practice, in most committee-based blockchains.Rdenotes the reward.

In addition, we assume that when an invalid block is produced, all opportunistic committee members incur a cost, which we denote byκ. Including an invalid block in the chain reduces the reputation and credibility of the chain and its business prospects and valuation, and thus the value of the coins staked by the committee members.κis borne by all the non adversary committee members, whether or not they voted for the block.

One can reasonably assume that the reputational cost is κ large and greater than the reward R . On the other hand, the reward R must be set above the cost C of verifying or generating a block. Otherwise, committee members would not be willing to participate in the committee. C , however, is larger than the cost c of sending a message. Checking validity requires significant internal computations and inspection of an entire sequence of blocks, while sending a message involves less and simpler computations. Thus, overall we assume κ κ > R > C > c > 0.

Another feature of the protocol is that when an invalid block is produced, the committee members who voted for the block are punished, which is referred to as “slashing.”22We write the cost of being slashed asS> 0. The interpretation is the following. To be part of the committee, coinholders must stake some of their coins; that is, they must deposit coins in an escrow account. Slashing means that the committee member loses partSof the deposit. If the information that the produced block is invalid and that the committee member voted for that block is contained in the blockchain, then slashing can be implemented with a smart contract.

Rewards and costs for adversaries

The adversaries have lexicographic preferences over the outcome of the game, in order they prefer:

Outcomes that do satisfy Termination, but not Validity;Outcomes that do not satisfy Termination;Outcomes that do satisfy Termination and Validity;

Adversaries are assumed to care only about the outcome of the protocol and neglect the costs of their own actions.

Equilibrium concept

Since we consider a dynamic game with asymmetric information, the relevant equilibrium concept is Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium (Fudenberg and Tirole 1991), intuitively defined as follows:

Definition 1. A Perfect Bayesian equilibrium is an n-tuple of strategies σ ( σ i ) i ∈ { 1 , … , n } ⁠ , and beliefs held by each player i about the strategies of the others σ σ − i such that all players (1) choose strategies that are optimal given their preferences and beliefs, (2) rationally anticipate the strategies of the others, and (3) draw rational inferences from what they observe, using their beliefs and Bayes law whenever it applies.

A Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium (PBE) is a Nash equilibrium (Nash 1951), so players best-respond to one another. It imposes additional restrictions, to take into account the fact that the game is dynamic and that players can have private information and must draw rational inferences. Rationality implies that each player’s beliefs are consistent with Bayes law, when computing probabilities conditional on events that have strictly positive probability on the equilibrium path. A PBE is perfect in the sense that strategies form a Bayesian equilibrium in every continuation game.
article.3 Equilibria:
3.1 The prescribed protocol is not an equilibrium

When a sufficiently large number of committee members are honest and follow the prescribed protocol, consensus obtains (seeAmoussou-Guenou et al. 2018). But what if committee members are self-interested economic agents, taking actions that are optimal for them given their preferences and beliefs? In the first step of our analysis, we examine if, for such agents, following the prescribed protocol can be an equilibrium strategy.

Recall that the prescribed protocol entails (a) proposing valid blocks, (b) verifying blocks, and (c) voting for valid blocks only. If the opportunistic committee members follow the prescribed protocol, consensus obtains: As long as the proposed block is invalid (because the leader was adversary) it does not get enough votes and is rejected. The first time the proposed block is valid, the number of committee members voting for the block isn–f, which is larger than the quorum,ν, so the block is produced.

Unfortunately, this is not an equilibrium because, when an opportunistic committee member anticipates the others follow the prescribed protocol, that committee member prefers to shirk on verification, to avoid bearing costC. To see this, consider opportunistic committee memberianticipating the other opportunistic committee members to follow the prescribed protocol:

Suppose round t is reached. Committee member i concludes that all previous leaders were adversaries. So the probability that the next leader is adversary is Pr ( adversary ) = f ( t ) − t n − t , where f ( t ) denotes the conditional expectation of f ˜ ⁠ , from the point of view of an opportunistic committee member knowing that all participants with indexes i ≤ t are adversaries.

At that stage, if committee member i follows the prescribed protocol and anticipates the other opportunistic committee members to do the same, it anticipates that a valid block (proposed by an opportunistic leader) will be produced with probability 1 − Pr ( adversary ) ⁠ , while, with the complementary probability the leader will be adversary, its block invalid and rejected and the protocol will move to the next round. So, if committee member i follows the prescribed protocol, its expected gain is − C + ( 1 − Pr ( adversary ) ) ( R − c ) + Pr ( adversary ) W i , t , where W i , t is the continuation value obtained by committee member i following the prescribed protocol at further rounds, like the other opportunistic committee members.

Now, consider the one-shot deviation in which committee member i deviates at round t by voting without verifying and then reverts to the prescribed protocol. She gets ( 1 − Pr ( adversary ) ) ( R − c ) + Pr ( adversary ) ( W i , t − c ) .

The first term corresponds to the gain of the deviating committee member when the leader was opportunistic and proposed a valid block. In that case again the block receives enough votes and is produced. The second term corresponds to the expected gain of the deviating committee members when the leader is adversary and proposes an invalid block. In that case, because the other opportunistic committee members follow the prescribed protocol, they verify the block, find out it is invalid and don’t vote for it. So the block is not produced, in spite of the deviation.

Comparing the expected payoff from following the prescribed protocol to the expected payoff from deviating, we have that committee member i prefers to deviate if C > Pr ( adversary ) c , which holds since C > c . So we can state our first proposition: 24

Proposition 1. It is not an equilibrium that all opportunistic committee members follow the prescribed protocol at all rounds. Proposition 1 obtains whatever the quorum ν , as long as it is strictly larger than f ¯ + 1 ⁠ . In particular the result holds even if ν ν = n − f ¯ so that (when the number of adversaries is f ¯ ⁠ ) the votes of all opportunistic committee members are needed for the valid block to be produced. While ν ν = n − f ¯ can make opportunistic committee members pivotal in the vote (if one of them fails to vote the valid block is not produced when the number of adversaries is f ¯ ⁠ ), it does not make them pivotal in verifications . Thus, opportunistic committee members free ride on verification, and the prescribed strategy is not an equilibrium.

Moreover, Proposition 1 obtains whatever the value of the slashing costS. When one opportunistic committee member anticipates the others follow the prescribed protocol, this committee member can deviate and shirk without running any risk of being slashed, because invalid blocks will be rejected.

3.2 Equilibria without consensus

Proposition 1 states that the prescribed protocol is not an equilibrium. But then what is an equilibrium? In this subsection, we show there exist equilibria in which consensus does not obtain, because validity or termination is not satisfied.

3.2.1 Equilibrium without validity

The next proposition presents an equilibrium in which invalid blocks can be produced.25

Proposition 2. If κ n − 1 n C − E ( f ) n κ < E ( f ) n S < min [ C + E ( f ) n ( R − c ) , R − c ] , (1) there exists an equilibrium in which (a) an opportunistic leader sends a valid block while an adversary sends an invalid block, (b) when receiving a block, opportunistic committee members vote for it without verifying it, and (c) adversaries vote in favor of invalid blocks only. Thus, in equilibrium, when the leader is opportunistic a valid block is produced, while when the leader is adversary an invalid block is produced.

In Proposition 2 there is termination, at the first round, but validity does not always hold. Opportunistic leaders send valid blocks, which end up produced.26But adversaries, propose invalid blocks and vote for these blocks, which end up produced.

Invalid blocks can be produced because opportunistic committee members vote for blocks without verifying them. Why do opportunistic committee members find it optimal to do so? Why don’t they prefer to verify blocks and vote only for valid blocks? As in Proposition 1, the problem arises because committee members are not pivotal.

At the first round, the expected payoff of an opportunistic committee member following the equilibrium strategy is κ R − c − E ( f ) n ( κ + S ) , reflecting that with probability E ( f ) n the leader is adversary, 27 in which case the block is invalid and yet produced, resulting in cost κ κ + S for the opportunistic committee member who voted for that block.

If the opportunistic committee member deviates from the equilibrium and follows the prescribed protocol, her expected payoff is κ − C − E ( f ) n κ + n − E ( f ) n ( R − c ) .

This expression reflects that, when the other committee members follow the equilibrium strategies of Proposition 2, an opportunistic committee member deviating to the prescribed behaviour is unable to alter the outcome of the protocol. Valid blocks still getn–fvotes and are produced, while invalid blocks getn– 1 votes and are also produced. In the latter case, the opportunistic committee member who followed the prescribed protocol incurs costκ, but not costS, since she is not slashed because she did not vote for the invalid block.

Comparing the expected payoff of the equilibrium strategy to that of (individually deviating to) the prescribed protocol, opportunistic committee members prefer to follow the equilibrium strategy if, E ( f ) n S < C + E ( f ) n ( R − c ) . (2)

The left-hand side of the inequality is the expected slashing cost from following the equilibrium strategy. The right-hand side is the verification cost, which committee members economize when following the equilibrium strategy rather than the prescribed protocol, plus the expected reward from invalid blocks, which committee members forgo when following the prescribed protocol.

Another possible deviation from the equilibrium strategy of Proposition 2 is to not vote at all. In that case, the opportunistic committee member gets κ − E ( f ) n κ ,

since her deviation is not enough to prevent invalid blocks from being accepted. This is lower than the equilibrium expected payoff if E ( f ) n S < R − c , (3) which states that the expected slashing cost is lower than the net reward the opportunistic committee member forgoes by not voting.

Finally, we need to examine under which condition an opportunistic leader prefers to propose a valid block. On the equilibrium path, the expected gain of an opportunistic leader is R − C − c .

If the opportunistic leader deviates by not proposing any block, the protocol moves to the next round. At that point, if the leader is opportunistic, a valid block is proposed and produced while if the leader is an adversary an invalid block is proposed and produced. So the expected gain of the deviating leader is κ R − c − E ( f ) n − 1 ( κ + S ) ,

where E ( f ) n − 1 is the probability that the second leader is an adversary. This is lower than the equilibrium payoff if κ n − 1 n C − E ( f ) n κ < E ( f ) n S . (4)

Putting together the three conditions(2),(3), and(4), opportunistic committee members prefer to follow the equilibrium strategy under condition(1).28In contrast with Proposition 1, Proposition 2 holds even if the quorumνis equal tof¯+1⁠. Whatever the value ofν, when all other opportunistic committee members vote without checking validity, a single committee member can never avoid the production of an invalid block, even if she checks validity and votes only for valid blocks.”

In Proposition 2 consensus fails because each opportunistic committee member finds it suboptimal to verify blocks. Verifying can be interpreted as producing a public good. Because they are not pivotal, opportunistic committee members free ride on the supply of this public good. Consequently, invalid blocks can be produced.

3.2.2 Equilibrium without termination

If slashing is very costly, condition(1)does not hold, and it cannot be expected that committee members vote without verification. Does that imply that when slashing is severe, consensus will arise in equilibrium? Proposition 1 implies that, even if slashing is severe, it cannot be expected either that all opportunistic committee members verify blocks and vote for valid blocks. This leaves the possibility that opportunistic committee members don’t vote. The next proposition states that this is indeed an equilibrium.29

Proposition 3. There exists an equilibrium in which opportunistic committee members never vote, so that no block is ever produced.

To see the logic of Proposition 3, consider an opportunistic committee member who receives a block and anticipates the others will not vote. This committee member anticipates that, even if she voted, no block would be produced, since the number of votes would still be below the quorum. Anticipating the others won’t vote, the opportunistic committee member prefers to also abstain from voting: its message would not change the outcome of the vote, and would only result in the committee member incurring costc. A fortiori, it is suboptimal for this committee member to verify validity, since it would only result in incurring costCwithout otherwise changing the outcome of the game.

In the equilibrium of Proposition 3, there is no termination. This reflects a coordination failure among committee members, who coordinate on a bad equilibrium in which no one votes. Votes, however, are observable. So lack of vote could be punished, by slashing. Such punishment, however, is not sufficient to induce termination. If an opportunistic committee member anticipates no opportunistic committee member to vote, then if lack of vote is punished by slashing, this committee member will not accept to participate in the committee, since participation can only entail punishments or costs but no rewards. If all opportunistic coinholders share this pessimistic expectation, none accepts to join the committee, which, again, rules out termination.

3.3 Equilibrium consensus

Our results, so far, are negative in the sense that they present equilibrium situations in which consensus isnotachieved or the prescribed strategy isnotfollowed. Is the committee-based protocol we examine doomed to fail? Fortunately, the answer to that question is negative. We hereafter show that there exists an equilibrium in which consensus is achieved.

To have termination and validity, it must be that sufficiently many opportunistic committee members prefer to verify blocks and vote only for valid blocks. The problem in Propositions 1 and 2 was that opportunistic committee members were tempted to free-ride, and let the others bear the verification cost. To avoid this situation, it must be that (at least some) opportunistic committee members are pivotal; that is, if they vote without verifying blocks, this can lead to producing invalid blocks, while if they verify blocks they can avoid the production of invalid blocks. In this section, we present an equilibrium in which this is the case.

3.3.1 Candidate equilibrium strategy

The first step of our construction of an equilibrium with consensus, is to define the candidate equilibrium strategyσσ∗⁠.

Definition 2. σ σ ∗ denotes opportunistic committee members’ strategy such that: When they are leaders: at rounds t ≤ f ¯ they propose valid blocks, at rounds f ¯ < t < n they don’t propose any block, and at round n they propose a valid block. When they receive blocks: committee members with index ν i > n − ν + f ¯ + 1 vote without verifying, committee members with index ν i ≤ n − ν + f ¯ + 1 verify the block and vote only for valid blocks at rounds t ≤ f ¯ ⁠ , and vote without verifying at rounds t > f ¯ ⁠ .

Under our assumption thatνσν>f¯+1,σ∗entails that some opportunistic members vote without verifying. In the limiting case in whichνσν=f¯+1,σ∗implies that all opportunistic members verify.

If the opportunistic committee members followσσ∗⁠, while adversaries propose invalid blocks and vote for invalid blocks, a valid block is eventually produced. Consensus therefore obtains. To see this more precisely, suppose opportunistic committee members follow strategyσσ∗⁠:

First; consider roundst≤f¯⁠: If the leader is opportunistic, a valid block is proposed and getsn–fvotes, which is above the quorumννso that the block is produced. If the leader is adversary, an invalid block is proposed. In that case, the number of opportunistic members voting for the block isννn−(n−ν+f¯+1)=ν−(f¯+1)⁠. So the total number of votes for the block isννν+f−(f¯+1)<ν⁠, and the block is not produced.

Second, turn to roundst>f¯⁠, still supposing opportunistic members followσσ∗⁠. The mere fact that we have reached these rounds imply thatf¯invalid blocks have already been proposed by adversaries. It implies that all the adversary committee members have already had the opportunity to propose a block, which implies that all committee members with indext>f¯are opportunistic. At roundn, the last potential leader prefers to propose a valid block, because it’s the last opportunity to produce a block and earn the corresponding net profit,R−C−c⁠.30At roundsf¯<t<n⁠, leaders prefer to do nothing. If they proposed a valid block they would earnR−C−cimmediately, while if they do nothing they earn a larger net profit,R–c, at roundn.

Our next proposition states there exists an equilibrium with consensus, in which opportunistic committee members followσσ∗⁠.31

Proposition 4. If R ≥ c + n − t + 1 n − f ( t ) C , ∀ t ≤ f ¯ + 1 , (5) and κ α β κ + S > max ( α ( t ) C − β ( t ) c , C / p ¯ ) , ∀ t < f ¯ , (6) there exists an equilibrium achieving consensus, in which adversaries propose invalid blocks and vote for invalid blocks, while opportunistic committee members follow strategy σ σ ∗ ⁠ .

To grasp the intuition of the proposition, note that condition(5)implies thatR(the reward for producing a block) is large relative to costscandC, which helps incentivizing leaders and voters, while condition(6)implies thatκκ+S(the cost of voting for an invalid block that is then produced) is large relative to costscandC, which helps incentivize verification. Further intuition on the incentives of the verifiers, and in particular the fact that in equilibrium they can be pivotal, is offered below:

At roundt≤f¯⁠, allt– 1 previous leaders were adversaries and the expected number of adversaries with index≥tisf(t−1)−(t−1)⁠. In this context, opportunistic committee members supposed to verify prefer to do so only if there run the risk of being pivotal. To examine this point, consider the maximum possible number of vote messages that can be sent if the roundtleader is adversary. In equilibrium, opportunistic committee members with indexes strictly larger thanνn−ν+f¯+1are to vote without verifying blocks. The worst-case scenario (maximizing the number of messages sent when the block is invalid) is that none of these committee members are adversaries and thatf=f¯⁠. In that case, the number of opportunistic committee members who vote without verifying isνν−f¯−1and the number of messages sent when the block is invalid isννf¯+(ν−f¯−1)=ν−1⁠. So, we narrowly escape validation of the invalid block. If, in that worst-case scenario one of the opportunistic committee members with index belowνn−ν+f¯+1deviated from equilibrium and sent a message without verifying the block, this would produce an invalid block. Thus, in that sense, the opportunistic committee members with index lower thanνn−ν+f¯+1are pivotal. In that context, they choose to verify blocks when(6)holds. The interpretation of(6)is that it implies the costκκ+Sof voting for an invalid block that ends up produced is so large that opportunistic committee members do not want to run the risk of tipping the balance. Condition(6)emphasizes the role of slashing in supporting consensus: Raising the slashing costSrelaxes constraint(6)by making the production of an invalid block more costly for the committee member.

One could worry that the equilibrium in Proposition 4 is complex, making it difficult for committee members to discover this equilibrium and coordinate on it. This difficulty can be circumvented by modifying the prescribed protocol. In the prescribed protocol, all committee members are instructed to verify blocks. We suggest a modification of the prescribed protocol requesting committee members to verify blocks if and only if their index is strictly lower thanνn−ν+f¯+2⁠. Proposition 4 implies that, when opportunistic processes expect the others to follow the modified prescribed protocol, their best response is to also follow the modified prescribed protocol. Thus, the modified prescribed protocol can be interpreted as a way to make equilibrium consensus a focal point, in the sense ofSchelling (1960).
article.4 Conclusion:
In public blockchains there is no central authority. The advantage is that this eliminates the risk of a malevolent central authority. The drawback is that this creates the risk of coordination failures and free riding. The present paper analyzes a protocol capturing the major features of committee-based blockchains, which are the major form of PoS blockchain. We show that this protocol admits equilibria in which free riding on costly but unobservable verification leads to the production of invalid blocks. We also show, however, there exists an equilibrium in which only valid blocks are produced. We suggest a modification of the prescribed protocol which implements this equilibrium.

Raising stakes, and correspondingly the amountSthat can be slashed, increases the parameter region for which there exists an equilibrium with consensus and reduces the parameter region for which there exists an equilibrium without validity. But staking is costly. Staked coins cannot be traded or lent, and they can be lost due to communication problems, hacks, or bugs. Moreover, requesting very large stakes restricts who can participate in the validation process, which is against the decentralization philosophy of public blockchains. This limits what can be expected from stake slashing. It is therefore necessary to also take into account other aspects of the workings of the protocol, such as pivotality in votes, which play a key role in the modification of the prescribed protocol we suggest.

Since the “Merge” of Ethereum, committee-based blockchains have become the major blockchain for Defi. So far, the new Ethereum protocol has been successful. Our analysis, however, underscores the risks that free riding and moral hazard problems could lead to insufficient verification effort and the production of invalid blocks. It is therefore important to monitor that risk and improve the protocol to mitigate it.

Proof of Proposition 4 The first step is to rule out possible deviations for adversaries. If an adversary deviated by sending a valid block, the block would be immediately produced, which would not make the adversary strictly better off. Moreover, since adversaries neglect the costs of verifying and sending messages, they weakly prefer to verify and send messages for invalid blocks, although, on the equilibrium path, these actions do not affect the outcome. The second step is to show that, when it is their turn to propose, opportunistic committee members prefer to propose a valid block. First, we note that opportunistic leaders prefer to propose a valid block rather than an invalid one. If the opportunistic committee member proposes a valid block, it is produced and the leader is rewarded, while if the opportunistic committee member proposes an invalid block, it is rejected, and we move to the next round, so the committee member gets less than its equilibrium gain. Second, we study whether opportunistic leaders prefer to propose a valid block than to propose no block. To do so, we proceed by backward induction. Suppose we reach round f ¯ + 1 ⁠ , which in equilibrium happens if f = f ¯ and all the committee members with the first f ¯ indexes are adversaries. At this point, in equilibrium, all the remaining leaders are opportunistic. They all anticipate that, if we reach round n , the leader at that point will be an opportunistic agent and will propose a valid block, giving them gain: R – c . If instead an opportunistic agent proposed a valid block before round n , it would get R − c − C ⁠ , which is lower. Thus, all agents with index strictly lower than n prefer not to propose any block, and the protocol moves to round n , at which a valid block is proposed and produced. Now, suppose we reach round f ¯ (which implies f ≥ f ¯ − 1 and all the previous leaders are adversaries). If the leader is adversary, it proposes an invalid block and we move to round f ¯ + 1 ⁠ . If instead the leader is opportunistic, in equilibrium it proposes a valid block and gets R − c − C . What would happen if the committee member deviated and did not generate a block? This is off the equilibrium path. Assume the out of equilibrium belief of the other opportunistic committee members is that the deviator was adversary, so that they believe all subsequent leaders will be opportunistic. From that point on, two scenarios are possible. p ¯ denotes the probability that the number of adversaries is f ¯ conditional on the f ¯ − 1 first leaders being adversary. With probability 1 − p ¯ ⁠ , all n − f ¯ − 1 remaining leaders are opportunistic, in which case the deviator earns R – c (at round n ). Alternatively, with probability p ¯ ⁠ , one of the remaining potential leaders is an adversary. When it is selected to propose a block, the adversary proposes an invalid block. Suppose the other committee members interpret this as stemming from an opportunistic agent. Then they vote for the block without verifying it, and the deviator gets R − c − κ κ − S ⁠ . Hence, the expected gain of the agent who deviated at round f ¯ is κ R − c − p ¯ ( κ + S ) . Consequently, the round f ¯ opportunistic leader is better off proposing a block than not proposing if κ R − C − c ≥ R − c − p ¯ κ . That is κ κ + S ≥ C / p ¯ , as implied by condition (6) . Finally, consider the case in which we reach round t < f ¯ ⁠ , because all leaders before t were adversaries. If the round t leader is adversary, it proposes an invalid block, which is not produced and we move to the next round. If instead the leader is opportunistic, in equilibrium it proposes a valid block which is produced. This gives the leader an equilibrium gain equal to R − C − c . If this agent deviated and did not propose any block, this would be an out of the equilibrium action, interpreted by the others as meaning that the leader was adversary. The committee members would afterwards follow the same behaviour as on the equilibrium path in the case in which the round t leader had been adversary.
